text: This video presents the surgical technique for an endoscopic assisted extra-elevator abdoperineal excision or ELAP.
An eighty-seven years old male patient presented a distal rectal cancer located at the level of the anorectal junction.
He had an ASA score of three and a BMI of twenty-nine.
The CT scan did not show any evidence of distant metastasis.
In addition, the MRI of the pelvis showed a tumor, stage MT-NN, located at the level of the anorectal junction.
Of note, the CRM was not treated.
Finally, the patient underwent a preoperative radiochemotherapy with
The MRI image displays the tumor located at the anorectal junction, indicated by an arrow.
The surgical procedure begins with the abdominal phase, where initial incisions and exposure of the abdominal cavity are performed.
The surgical team initiates the procedure using a laparoscopic approach, employing a setup that includes a twelve-millimeter camera and three five-millimeter ports.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, ligating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at a high level, which is visually observable as the artery is isolated and secured.
The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, advancing anteriorly to the level of the vesicles and posteriorly down to the Valderia fascia.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the ureters and hypogastric nerves during the dissection, ensuring these structures remain intact.
The surgeon begins dissecting the peritoneum on the medial side, performing a classical medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the left ureter during the rectal cancer procedure, ensuring it remains intact amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using Hemolock clips, employing the standard technique for vessel ligation.
The surgeon performs further dissection in the pelvis, opening the peritoneum on the lateral side of the rectum, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane and continues dissection downwards towards the Valdir fascia, ensuring the plane is followed as deeply as possible.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum on the left side of the rectum using a hook, continuing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon divides the lateral attachments of the rectum using a hook, continuing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum anteriorly using a hook, continuing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon continues posterior dissection of the rectum, encountering difficulty due to tissue changes from previous radiotherapy.
The surgeon continues the anterior dissection, revealing the vesicles in the surgical field.
As we can see, the pelvis was quite narrow and the patient almost obese, so the dissection was quite difficult in this area.
The video displays the current level achieved in the abdominal dissection, highlighting the extent of tissue separation and anatomical exposure.
The surgeon begins the perineal phase of the procedure, closing the anus and performing an extra sphincter dissection.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection starting at the level of the coccyx, followed by an anterior dissection, and concludes with a lateral dissection, adhering to the ELAB principles.
The image illustrates the ELAB procedure, highlighting the extrasyncteric and extralevator dissection techniques being applied in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the perineal phase by closing the anus, preparing for the extra sphincter dissection.
The surgeon opens the skin, initiating the perineal phase of the procedure following the closure of the anus and extra sphincter dissection.
The surgeon applies a Ditamine and a Lone Star retractor to achieve effective retraction of the tissue, enhancing visibility in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an extra sphincteric dissection, following the ELAB principles, with visible dissection around the sphincter area.
The surgeon applies a running suture along the edge of the skin, preparing the area before placing the surgical port.
It's very important to have a very good sealing during the procedure.
The surgeon places the gel point port and ties the suture to ensure a secure sealing at the incision site.
The surgeon initiates pneumopelvis, inflating the pelvic cavity to an initial pressure of twelve mmHg.
The surgeon initiates the dissection posteriorly at the level of the coccyx, focusing on the anatomical area behind the rectum.
Usually, it is not required to remove it, but it can be done if required.
The surgeon searches for the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, aiming to identify the correct anatomical layer for dissection.
The surgeon follows the principles of total mesorectal excision (TME) to continue the dissection, maintaining the surgical plane established earlier.
Obviously, good skills in TEM and single-side surgery is mandatory for this procedure.
The surgeon transects the pelvic floor laterally using the principles of the ILA procedure, ensuring the incision is as lateral as possible.
The surgeon transects the muscles on the right side of the pelvic floor, adhering to the ELAP principles, with visible separation of muscle fibers.
We think that this endoscopic assisted ELAP procedure offers very good view on the transection of the muscles.
In addition, the patient can stay
on his back the legs up and doesn't need to go on the prone position.
The surgeon follows the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, continuing cranially to connect with the previously dissected area.
The surgeon uses a hook to completely divide the levator muscles on the left side, following the principles of the ELAP procedure.
The most difficult part is the anterior plane.
The surgeon resumes dissection using a hook instrument, focusing on the anatomical planes involved in the rectal cancer procedure.
One of the disadvantages of this endoscopic assisted procedure is the stability of the pneumopelvis.
The surgeon performs a lateral dissection to prevent working in a deep hole, ensuring better access and visibility in the surgical field.
Another disadvantage remains the risk of smoke that could render the procedure quite painful.
The surgeon continues the anterior dissection, carefully navigating around the prostate while minimizing the risk of bleeding and nerve injury.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection, joining the efforts with the abdominal team to ensure continuity of the surgical approach.
The abdominal team assists by retracting the specimen using a suction device, providing better visibility and access for the ongoing procedure.
The surgeon reopens the anterior dissection plane, establishing visual communication with the abdominal team to coordinate further surgical actions.
The surgeon divides the last lateral attachments using a ligature device, completing the separation of the rectal specimen from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon extracts the completely freed specimen through the anus, using an Alexi wound protector to safeguard the surrounding tissue during removal.
The surgeon checks for hemostasis and performs a full washout of the surgical area, ensuring no active bleeding is present.
The surgeon places a routine drain in the pelvis, which exits through one of the five millimeter laparoscopic ports.
The surgeon forms an end colostomy and closes the perineal wound in several layers, completing the final steps of the procedure.
The total operative time was six hour and a half.
There was no complication.
We had a complete TME specimen and the final pathology showed a YPT two and zero with eleven lymph nodes found.
The margins were clear including the CRM with a minimum of six millimeter.
